question: Hoysala monuments are found in........... (UPSC Prelims 2001)
option_1: Hampi and Hospet
option_2: Halebid and Belur
option_3: Mysore and Bangalore
option_4: Sringeri and Dharwar
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Hoysala monuments are found in Halebid and Belur. In September 2023, the Hoysala temples at Belur, Halebid, and Somanathapur in Karnataka were declared UNESCO World Heritage Sites.

question:
Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 2001)

(a) Harappan Civilisation

 Painted Grey Ware

(b) The Kushans

 Gandhara School of Art

(c) The Mughals

 Ajanta Paintings

(d) The Marathas

 Pahari School of Painting
option_1:
Harappan Civilisation

 Painted Grey Ware
option_2:
The Kushans

 Gandhara School of Art
option_3:
The Mughals

 Ajanta Paintings
option_4:
The Marathas

 Pahari School of Painting
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The correct pair is:

  • The Kushans : Gandhara School of Art
  • The Kushan Empire, which existed from the 1st to the 3rd century CE in the region of present-day Afghanistan, Pakistan, and Northern India, was known for its patronage of the Gandhara School of Art. The Gandhara School of Art flourished in the Gandhara region (modern-day Pakistan and Afghanistan) during the Kushan period. It was heavily influenced by Hellenistic and Roman artistic styles and produced iconic sculptures and reliefs depicting Buddhist themes.

The other pairs mentioned are not correctly matched:

  • Harappan Civilization is associated with the Indus Valley Civilization, which thrivedaround 2600 to 1900 BCE in the region of present-day Pakistan and Western India. The Painted Grey Ware is associated with the later Vedic period (around 1000 to 600 BCE) and is not related to the Harappan Civilization.
  • The Mughals were a dynasty that ruled much of the Indian subcontinent from the 16th to the 19th century. They were not associated with the Ajanta Paintings. The Ajanta Caves, located in Maharashtra, India, are renowned for their ancient Buddhist rock-cut cave paintings and sculptures that date back to the 2nd century BCE to 6th century CE.
  • The Marathas were a prominent regional power in Western India during the 17th and 18th centuries. They were not associated with the Pahari School of Painting. The Pahari School of Painting refers to a style of miniature painting that developed in the hilly regions of Himachal Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir, and Uttarakhand in Northern India.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists: (UPSC Prelims 2001)

List I

 List II

(Dancer)

(Dance)

A. Kalamandalam v Kshemavaty

 1. Kathakali

B.Kottakkal Sivaraman

 2. Manipuri

C.Lakshmi Viswanathan

 3.Mohiniyattam

D. N. Madhabi Devi

 4.Bharatanatyam

Codes : A B C D
option_1: 1 3 2 4
option_2: 3 1 4 2
option_3: 1 3 4 2
option_4: 3 1 2 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kalamandalam Kshemavathy is a Mohiniyattam dancer from Thrissur,Kerala. Mohiniyattam is a semiclassical dance form from Kerala, India that combines Bharatnatyam and Kathakali. It’s performed by women in honor of the Hindu god Vishnu in his incarnation as the enchantress Mohini.
  • Kalamandalam Kshemavathy is related to Mohiniyattam. She joined the Kerala Kalamandalam when she was 11 years old and learned Bharatanatyam and Mohiniyattam. She completed her Diploma in Indian classical dance (Bharatanatyam and Mohiniyattam) from Kerala Kalamandalam.
  • Kottakkal Sivaraman (1936 – 19 July 2010) was a performing artiste who revolutionised the portrayal of female roles in Kathakali, the classical dance-drama from Kerala in southern India.
  • Bharatnatyam is also known as Dashiattam. Lakshmi Vishwanathan is related to Bharatnatyam dance.
  • N. Madhabi Devi is a renowned singer and performer of the Manipuri Ras tradition and Nata-sankirtana. Manipuri is a classical dance form from the northeastern Indian state of Manipur. It is based on Hindu scriptures and is associated with the worship of Radha and Krishna. The most popular forms of Manipuri dance are Ras, Sankirtana, and Thang-Ta.

question: Chapchar Kut is a festival celebrated in the state of........... (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: Arunachal Pradesh
option_2: Assam
option_3: Mizoram
option_4: Sikkim
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Chapchar Kut is a spring festival celebrated in the state of Mizoram, India. The festival is celebrated in March after the completion of jhum cultivation, which is the process of growing crops by clearing land of trees and vegetation and burning them. The festival is estimated to have started between 1450 1700 A.D. in the village of Suaipui, and wasrevived in 1962.
  • The festival is a colorful welcome of the spring season. It is Mizoram’s biggest and most important festival, and attracts thousands of people. During the festival, Mizo people wear colorful traditional attire and makeup, and dance to the music of bands and songs for the Cheraw and Chai dances.

question: Which one of the following statements is not correct?  (UPSC Prelims 2002)
option_1: The status of Gomateshwara at Sravanabelagola represents the last Tirthankara of Jains
option_2: India’s largest Buddhist monastery is in Arunachal Pradesh
option_3: The Khajuraho temples were built under Chandela Kings
option_4: The Hoysalesvara temple is dedicated to Siva
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The statement “The status of Gomateshwara at Sravanabelagola represents the last Tirthankara of Jains” is not correct.
  • The Gomateshwara statue, also known as Bahubali statue, is a 57-foot (17 m) high monolithic statue on Vindhyagiri Hill in the town of Shravanbelagola in the Indian state of Karnataka. It is a significant Jain pilgrimage site. The statue was built in deference to Jain deity Bahubali, who is said to have lived many millions of years ago.
  • Tawang Monastery is the largest Buddhist monastery in India and is located in the valley of the Tawang Chu in Arunachal Pradesh. It is located near the Chinese and Bhutanese border. Tawang is a major holy site for Tibetan Buddhists because it is the birthplace of the sixth Dalai Lama.
  • The Chandela dynasty built the Khajuraho temples between 950 and 1050. The Chandela dynasty ruled the region of present-day Madhya Pradesh, India, from the 9th to 13th centuries.
  • The Chandela dynasty’s construction activity began almost immediately afterthey rose to power. The construction of the Khajuraho temples was initiated by the Chandela ruler, King Yasovarman. Most temples were built during the reigns of the Hindu kings Yashovarman and Dhanga.
  • The Khajuraho temple site had 85 temples by the 12th century, spread over 20 square kilometers. Of these, only about 25 temples have survived, spread over six square kilometers.
  • The Chandela dynasty is a Rajput clan found in North India. They are regarded to be Chandravanshi, which means the descendants of Soma.
  • The Hoysaleshwara temple in Hoysaleswara, India is dedicated to Lord Shiva. It’s also known as the Halebidu temple.
  • The temple was built between 1120 and 1150 by King Vishnuvardhana during the reign of the Hoysala Empire. It’s a double temple, with each side having a sanctum that contains a linga. Lingas are non-figural forms with a rounded top that represent Shiva’s divine presence.
  • The exterior walls of the temple are divided into three parts, with the bottom part featuring eight sculptural friezes. The friezes depict elephants, lions, vines, horses, narratives, makaras, and hamsas.
  • The temple also has many sculptures dedicated to Vaishnavism.

question:
Consider the following statements:  (UPSC Prelims 2003)

1. Kitab-I-Nauras, a collection of songs in praise of Hindu deities and Muslim saints, was written by Ibrahim Adil Shah II.

2. Amir Khusrau was the originator in India of the early form of the musical style known as Qawali.

Which of these statements is/are correct?
option_1: Only 1
option_2: Only 2
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Kitab-I-Nauras is a collection of songs in praise of Hindu deities and Muslim saints written by Ibrahim Adil Shah II.
  • Ibrahim Adil Shah II was a king of the Bijapur Sultanate and a member of the Adil Shahi dynasty in the late 16th century.
  • He was also a ruler who was deeply interested in music. Kitab-I-Nauras is a book of 59 songs and 17 couplets written in Dakhni Urdu. The songs are set to various musical modes or Ragas. The book also includes the theory of nine Ragas, which is important in Indian aesthetics.
  • Some verses in Kitab-I-Nauras explore love poetry, while others describe the beauty of music or ragas as personifications. The book also includes verses where Ibrahim refers to Ganesha and Saraswati as his spiritual mother and father.
  • Amir Khusrau is known as the “father of qawwali”. Qawwali is a devotional singing style of the Sufis in the Indian subcontinent. Khusrau is also credited with introducing the ghazal style of song into India. Both styles are still widely practiced in India and Pakistan.
  • Khusrau was a 13th century Sufi musician, poet, and scholar associated with the royal courts of emperors. He is known for his prolific output, introducing many new genres into the North Indian classical tradition. Khusrau’s original songs are still the core of the classical qawwali repertoire.

question: Mrichchhakatika’ an ancient Indian book written by Shudraka deals with............. (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: The love affair of a rich merchant with the daughter of a courtesan
option_2: The victory of Chandragupta II over the Shaka Kshatrapas of western India
option_3: The military expeditions and exploits of Samudragupta
option_4: The love affair between a Gupta King and a princess of Kamarupa
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Mrichchhakatika is a Sanskrit play written by Shudraka in the 2nd century BCE. Itis about the love affair between a wealthy merchant and the daughter of a courtesan. The play is also known as The Little Clay Cart.
  • The play is about a young man named Charudatta and his love for Vasantasena, a wealthy courtesan. However, the couple’s lives and love are threatened when a vulgar courtier begins to aggressively pursue Vasantasena.
  • The play’s main themes include infidelity and dishonesty. It also includes other themes, such as poverty.

question: Which one of the following statements if NOT correct? (UPSC Prelims 2003)
option_1: The National Song Vande Mataram was composed by Bankimchandra Chatterji originally in Bengali
option_2: The National Calendar of India based on Saka era has its 1st Chaitra on 22nd March normally and 21st March in a leap year
option_3: The design of the National Flag of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 22nd July, 1947
option_4: The song Jana-gana-mana’, composed originally in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore was adopted in its Hindi version by the Constituent Assembly on 24th January, 1950 as the national Anthem of India
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Bankim Chandra Chatterjee composed the national song of India, Vande Mataram, in Sanskrit and Sanskritized Bengali in the 1870s.
  • The poem was included in his 1882 Bengali novel Anandamath. In 1896, Rabindranath Tagore first sang the poem at the Indian National Congress session. In 1937, the Congress adopted the first two verses of the poem as the national song of India.
  • Vande Mataram was a source of inspiration for Indians during the struggle for freedom. It has equal status with Jana-gana-mana.
  • The national calendar based on the Saka Era, with Chaitra as its first month and a normal year of 365 days was adopted from 22 March 1957 along with the Gregorian calendar.
  • The Constituent Assembly of India adopted the design of the national flag on July 22, 1947. The flag became official when India became a republic.
  • The flag is a horizontal tricolour with equal proportions of deep saffron (Kesari) at the top, white in the middle, and dark green at the bottom. In the center of the white band is a navy-blue wheel with 24 spokes that represents the chakra. The wheel’s design is based on the wheel that appears on the abacus of the Sarnath Lion Capital of Ashoka.
  • The Ashoka Chakra was suggested by B.R. Ambedkar to honor the warrior-turned pacifist Buddhist emperor Ashoka. The chakra was modeled after the “wheel of dharma,” a religious motif from Hinduism, Jainism, and especially Buddhism.
  • The National Anthem of India Jana-gana mana, composed originally in Bengali by Rabindranath Tagore, was adopted in its Hindi version by the Constituent Assembly as the National Anthem of India on 24 January 1950. It was first sung on 27 December 1911 at the Kolkata Session of the Indian National Congress.

question: The Archaeological Survey of India is an attached office of the Department/ Ministry of............ (UPSC Prelims 2004)
option_1: Culture
option_2: Tourism
option_3: Science and Technology
option_4: Human Resource Development
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) is an attached office of the Ministry of Culture. The ASI was established in 1861 and is the leading organization for archaeological research and the protection of India’s cultural heritage.
  • The Ministry of Culture is responsible for the preservation and promotion ofIndia’s art and culture. The Ministry’s work includes generating cultural awareness at the grassroots level and promoting cultural exchanges internationally.
  • The ASI sends and receives experts in the fields of archaeological conservation, preservation, museology, and field archaeology.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2004)

1. The Islamic Calendar is twelve days shorter than the Gregorian Calendar.

2. The Islamic Calendar began in AD 632.

3. The Gregorian Calendar is a solar calendar.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 1 and 2
option_3: 1 and 3
option_4: 3 only
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Islamic calendar, also known as the Hijri calendar, is about 11–12 days shorter than the Gregorian calendar. This is because the Islamic calendar is based on the lunar cycle, while the Gregorian calendar is based on the solar cycle.
  • The Islamic calendar is a lunar calendar, with 12 months that are based on the cycles of the moon. A Hijri month alternates between 29 and 30 days, making a Hijri year 354–355 days long. The Gregorian calendar, on the other hand, is based on the solar cycle and has 365–366 days.
  • The Islamic calendar shifts by about 11 days every year with respect to the Gregorian calendar. This is because 12 lunar months are only 354. 36 days long.
  • The Islamic calendar, also known as the Hijri calendar, began in 622 CE. The Islamic New Year is celebrated in 622 CE, the year when the Prophet Muhammad and his followers migrated from Mecca to Medina. The word “Hijra” is Arabic for “migration”.

question:
Consider the following statements:  (UPSC Prelims 2005)

1. Champaner-Pavagadh Archaeological Park

2. Chhatrapati Shivaji Railway Station, Mumbai

3. Mamallapuram

4. Sun Temple (Konark Temple)

Which of the above are included in the World Heritage List of UNESCO :
option_1: 1, 2 and 3
option_2: 1, 3 and 4
option_3: 2 and 4
option_4: 1, 2, 3 and 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

The Champaner - Pavagadh Archaeological Park :

  • It is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in the Panchmahal district of Gujarat, India. It features a variety of archaeological, historical, and cultural heritage properties.
  • The park is located around the historical city of Champaner, which was founded in the eighth century by Vanraj Chavda, the king of the Chavda Dynasty. The Pavagadh hill was a famous Hindu fortress under the Solanki kings of Gujarat, followed by Khichi Chauhans.

Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus, also known as VT or CSMT :

  • It is a historic railway station in Mumbai, India. It’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site and one of the cleanest stations on Indian Railways.
  • The station was designed by British architect Frederick William Stevens and Axel Haig in the Italian Gothic style. Construction began in 1878 and the station opened on June 20, 1887. The station is an example of Victorian Gothic Revival architecture, blending with Indian traditional architecture.
  • It’s also known for its decorations, which include a statue of progress, busts of the railway company’s directors, and statues of a British lion.

Mamallapuram :

  • It is also known as Mahabalipuram, is a city in Tamil Nadu, India. It is located on a strip of land between the Bay of Bengal and the Great Salt Lake. 
  • Mamallapuram is known for its temples and monuments built by the Pallava dynasty in the 7th and 8th centuries. The monuments are an example of Dravidian temple architecture and Pallava art. The Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram is a UNESCO World Heritage site. 

The Konark Sun Temple :

  • It is a 13th-century Hindu temple in Konark, Odisha, India. It’s a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is known for its architecture and stone carvings.
  • The temple is dedicated to the Hindu sun god Surya, and is an example of Kalinga architecture. It’s about 35 kilometers northeast of Puri city on the coastline.
  • The temple’s name comes from the Sanskrit words Kona (angle) and Arka (sun). The temple features 24 wheels, representing the wheels of Surya’s chariot. The 12 wheels represent the 12 months of the year, and the 8 spokes represent the 8 time divisions of the day.

question: Recently, to which of the following countries did India offer to build a Buddha Temple? (UPSC Prelims 2005)
option_1: China
option_2: Myanmar
option_3: Thailand
option_4: Vietnam
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • India recently offered to build a Buddhist temple in China’s Henan province. The temple will be located on the western side of the White Horse Temple in Luoyang.
  • The temple is a gift to the Chinese people and is inspired by the Sanchi Stupa. It took five years to build and cost $4 million. The temple is a fusion of ancient design principles and modern technology.
  • The offer is a sign of the cultural connection between the two countries. The White Horse Temple was the first Buddhist temple built in China, in 67 AD.

question: Who among the following is a Hindustani classical singer? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Geeta Chandran
option_2: Leela Samson
option_3: Gangubai Hangal
option_4: Swapnasundari
solution_text:

question:
Which one of the following pairs is correctly matched? (UPSC Prelims 2006)

(a) Vikramsila

Monastery

 Uttar Pradesh

(b) Hemkund

Gurudwara

 Himachal Pradesh

(c) Udayagiri Caves

 Maharashtra

(d) Amaravati

Buddhist Stupa

Andhra Pradesh
option_1:
Vikramsila Monastery

 Uttar Pradesh
option_2:
Hemkund Gurudwara

Himachal Pradesh
option_3:
Udayagiri Caves

 Maharashtra
option_4:
Amaravati Buddhist Stupa

Andhra Pradesh
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

Vikramashila :

  • It was a Buddhist monastery and one of the most important centers of learning in India during the Pala Empire. The monastery is located at the site of Antichak village, Bhagalpur district in Bihar.
  • The monastery was constructed between the 9th and 12th centuries AD. The monastery is a huge square structure with 208 cells, 52 on each side, that open into a common verandah. The monastery gradually became a center for Tantric Buddhism.
  • The Vikramshila Museum is located 36 km east of the Bhagalpur district in the village of Antichak. The museum was established in 2004 and exhibits antiquities recovered from excavations by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI). According to legend, Khilji forces mistook the university for a fortress and burned it down.
  • The site was forgotten for centuries until Lakshmikanta Mishra discovered bricks and structures in a mound at Antichak Village.

Hemkund Sahib :

  • It is also known as Gurudwara Sri Hemkunt Sahib Ji, is a Sikh place of worship and pilgrimage site in Chamoli, Uttarakhand, India. It’s located at an altitude of around 4,329 meters on the banks of Hemkund Lake.
  • Hemkund Sahib is dedicated to Guru Gobind Singh, the tenth Sikh Guru, and is mentioned in the Dasam Granth. It’s believed that Guru Gobind Singh spent 10 years meditating at the site. For many Sikhs, visiting Hemkund Sahib is a way to connect with their guru and receive his blessings.
  • Hemkund Sahib is a popular center of Sikh pilgrimage, with thousands of devotees visiting each summer.

The Udayagiri Caves :

  • They are a complex of 20 rock-cut caves in Vidisha, Madhya Pradesh. The caves are primarily Hindu temple architecture from the early 3rd century CE to the 5th century CE. They are known for being some of India’s oldest Hindu structures and contain some of the oldest surviving Hindu temples and iconography. The caves are also known as the Sunrise Caves and are a stunning representation of the Gupta Empire’s architectural and artistic achievements.

The Amaravati Stupa :

  • It is a ruined Buddhist stupa in the village of Amaravathi, Palnadu district, Andhra Pradesh, India. It was likely built in phases between the third century BCE and about 250 CE. The stupa was one of the biggest in Andhra Pradesh with a probable diameter of 50 meters and a height of 27 meters.
  • The stupa was an important monastic center until the 14th century AD. It fell into disuse and was covered in dust and debris over time. In 1797, Colonel Colin Mackenzie discovered and made the stupa famous. After its discovery, several European officials showed interest in the sculptures found at the site and even tried to excavate the structure. The Amaravati Stupa is protected by the archaeological Survey of India.

question: The initial design and construction of which massive temple took place during the reign of Suryavarman II? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Sri Mariamman Temple
option_2: Angkor Vat
option_3: Batu Caves Temple
option_4: Kamakhya Temple
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The Angkor Wat temple in Cambodia :

  • It was built during the reign of King Suryavarman II, who ruled from 1113 c. 1150. The temple’s initial design and construction took place from 1122–1150 CE, over a period of 28 years.
  • The temple is dedicated to Lord Vishnu and is the world’s largest religious structure. Suryavarman II, a religious reformer and temple builder, intended the temple to be the supreme abode for Vishnu. The temple is a break from the Shaiva tradition of previous kings.

Sri Mariamman Temple :

  • It is a Hindu temple in Singapore’s Chinatown district. It’s the oldest Hindu temple in Singapore, built in 1827 by immigrants from South India. The temple is dedicated to Goddess Mariamman, who is known for her ability to cure epidemic illnesses and diseases.

The Batu Caves Temple :

  • It is also known as Sri Subramaniar Swamy Temple, is the main attraction of the Batu Caves. It is a Hindu temple and tourist attraction with intricate carvings and sculptures. Some say the temple is a focal point for spiritual worship and cultural celebrations.

The Kamakhya Temple at Nilachal hills in Guwahati, Assam :

  • It is one of the oldest and most revered centres of Tantric practices, dedicated to the goddess Kamakhya. The temple is the center of the Kulachara Tantra Marga and the site of the Ambubachi Mela, an annual festival that celebrates the menstruation of the goddess.
  • Structurally, the temple is dated to the 8th-9th century with many subsequent rebuildings and the final hybrid architecture defines a local style called Nilachal. It is also one among the oldest 4 of the 51 pithas in the Shakta tradition.

question: Who among the following was presented with the Sangeet Natak Akadami Ratna Award, 2005 for her lifetime achievement in dance? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Chandralekha
option_2: Tanjavur Balasaraswati
option_3: Uma Sharma
option_4: Yamini Krishnamurthy
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Chandralekha Prabhudas Patel, also known as Chandralekha, received the Sangeet Natak Akademi Ratna Award in 2005 for her lifetime achievement in dance. Chandralekha is an Indian dancer and choreographer.
  • The Sangeet Natak Akademi is India’s National Academy of Music, Dance, and Drama. It was established in 1952 to promote and preserve India’s cultural heritage through dance, drama, and music.
  • The Sangeet Natak Akademi Award, also known as the “Akademi Puraskar”, is the highest national recognition in the performing arts.

question: Which one of the following revolts was made famous by Bankim Chandra Chatterjee in his novel Anand Math? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Bhil uprising
option_2: Rangpur and Dinapur uprising
option_3: Bishnupur and Birbhum rebellion
option_4: Sanyasi rebellion
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Bankim Chandra Chattopadhyay’s 1882 novel Ananda Math is based on the Sannyasi Rebellion, which took place from 1763 1800. The novel is considered a significant work in Bengali and Indian literature, and its first English publication was titled The Abbey of Bliss.
  • The Sannyasi Rebellion was a civil uprising led by religious monks and dispossessed zamindars. The rebellion was a response to the famine of 1770 and the British East India Company’s harsh economic policies. The Sanyasis fought back by attacking thecompany’s factories and state depositories. The rebellion was eventually crushed under the leadership of Warren Hastings.
  • Ananda Math also includes the song “Vande Mataram”, which is considered India’s national song.

The Bhil uprising :

  • It was a tribal uprising that took place in 1818 in the Dhula district of Maharashtra. The uprising was a response to the East India Company’s exploitation and denial of traditional forest rights to the Bhil community. The Bhil uprising was one of the first uprisings by a tribal group in India.
  • The British responded to the uprising by sending a force to suppress the rebellion. As part of the peace settlement, the British gave concessions to various taxes and returned forest rights.
  • The Bhils continued their uprisings until 1838, when a massive uprising took place and was subdued by force. In 1879, the Bhils revolted again by killing some moneylenders who had exploited them economically.

The Rangpur Dhing :

  • It was a rebellion that took place in the Rangpur district of Bengal in 1783. The rebellion was a protest by peasants and zamindars against the East India Company’s high revenue demands. The uprising began on January 18, 1783, and lasted for five weeks.
  • The rebellion is considered the first major uprising against Company rule in Bengal. The rebellion brought to light the weaknesses of the ijardari system, and the government made some reforms to the farming system.
  • The rebellion started under the leadership of Dhiraj Narayan.
  • The peasants and zamindars took control of the Parganas of Kakina, Kazirhat, and Tepa. They attacked courts, looted grains, and released prisoners. The uprising also spread to Dinajpur.

Bishnupur Rebellion :

  • In 1787, Bishnupur and Birbhum were united to form a separate administrative unit, with the headquarters moved to Suri. This led to a rebellious situation.
  • In 1788–1790, the peasant masses joined bandits in a long and violent rebellion against the East India Company’s oppressive rule. By November 1789, the English had lost control of Bishnupur, and the East India Company had suffered significant financial losses.
  • Towards the end of the 18th century, parts of the district were affected by the Chuar rebellion. The Chuar Rebellion was at its peak in 1798–99 under the leadership of Durjan Singh, Lal Singh, and Mohan Singh, but was crushed by the British Company’s forces.
  • Bishnupur is a city and municipality in the Bankura district of West Bengal, India. It is known for its terracotta temples, historic Radha Krishna temples, and the Baluchari sarees.

question: Between which of the following was the ancient town of Takshasila located? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Indus and Jhelum
option_2: Jhelum and Chenab
option_3: Chenab and Ravi
option_4: Ravi and Beas
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The ancient city of Takshasila, also known as Taxila, was located between the Indus and Jhelum rivers. It is located in what is now northwestern Pakistan.
  • Takshasila was a significant center of learning and an important city in the Mauryan and Gupta Empires. It was part of the Indus Valley Civilization, which spread across present-day Pakistan and northwest India. In 1980, Takshasila was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: Lectures from Colombo to Almora is based on the experiences of which one of the following? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Veer Savarkar
option_2: Annie Besant
option_3: Ramkrishna Paramhansa
option_4: Swami Vivekanand
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Lectures from Colombo to Almora is based on the experiences of Swami Vivekananda. The book was published in 1897 and is a collection of 29 speeches he delivered in India and Sri Lanka after returning from the West.
  • Swami Vivekananda was a Hindu spiritual leader and reformer who was born in 1863 and died in 1902. He is known for introducing Vedanta and Yoga to the West through his writings and speeches. His works, including Lectures from Colombo to Almora and Raja Yoga, explore spirituality, self-realization, and the unity of religions.

question: The Allahabad Pillar inscription is associated with which one of the following? (UPSC Prelims 2006)
option_1: Mahapadma Nanda
option_2: Chandragupta Maurya
option_3: Ashoka
option_4: Samudragupta
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Allahabad Pillar is associated with the Mauryan emperor Ashoka, who reigned in the 3rd century BCE. Ashoka erected the pillar to inscribe his edicts about the spread of Buddhism. Later, Samudragupta used the pillar to praise his own achievements, conquests, and character.
  • The Allahabad Pillar Inscription is a 4th century panegyric of Samudragupta, written in Sanskrit prose and verse. The inscription describes the king’s virtues and military exploits, and mentions that rulers of several frontier kingdoms and tribal oligarchies paid tribute to Samudragupta. The inscription also mentions the Davaka kingdom, one of five frontier kingdoms of the Gupta Empire.

question: Where is the famous Vijayavitala temple having its 56 carved pillars emitting musical notes located? (UPSC Prelims 2007)
option_1: Belur
option_2: Bhadrachalam
option_3: Hampi
option_4: Srirangam
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Vijaya Vittala Temple, also known as the Vittala Temple, is located in Hampi, Karnataka. It’s near the banks of the Tungabhadra River in the northeastern part of Hampi.
  • The temple is dedicated to Lord Vitthala, an incarnation of Lord Vishnu. It was built in the 15th century AD during the reign of King Devaraya II (1422 - 1446 AD).
  • The temple is famous for its 56 musical pillars, each 3.6 meters high, that emit musical notes when gently tapped. The pillars are known as SaReGaMa pillars, and the temple emits seven musical notes when tapped.
  • The temple is a UNESCO World Heritage Site and is known for its architecture and craftsmanship.

question: Who among the following wrote the book Babuvivah? (UPSC Prelims 2007)
option_1: Raja Rammohan Roy
option_2: Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar
option_3: Pandita Rambai
option_4: Rabindranath Tagore

  • Vidyasagar was a Bengali educator and social reformer from the Indian state of West Bengal.

question: Near Which one of the following cities are Palitana Temples located? (UPSC Prelims 2008)
option_1: Bhavnagar
option_2: Mount Abu
option_3: Nasik
option_4: Ujjain
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Palitana temples are located near the city of Palitana in the Bhavnagar district of Gujarat, India. The temples are located on Shatrunjaya hills, about 50 kilometers southwest of Bhavnagar city.
  • The Palitana temples are a complex of nearly 900 Jain temples, including the main temple dedicated to Rishabhanatha. The complex is one of the most sacred sites in the Svetambara tradition of Jainism, and is considered a major pilgrimage center for Jains. The earliest temples in the complex date back to the 11th century.

question: What is the number of spokes in the Dharma chakra in hte National Flag of India?  (UPSC Prelims 2008)

  •  The Indian flag has three horizontal stripes: saffron at the top, white in the middle, and green at the bottom. The Ashoka Chakra is in the center of the white stripe. The Ashoka Chakra replaced the charkha (spinning wheel) symbol on the pre-independence versions of the flag.

question: Where is the famous Virupaksha temple located?  (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Bhadrachalam
option_2: Chidambaram
option_3: Hampi
option_4: Srikalahasti
solution_text:

  • The Virupaksha Temple is located in Hampi, Karnataka, India. It is part of the Group of Monuments at Hampi, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
  • The temple is dedicated to Lord Shiva and his consort Parvati. It is one of the oldest temples in India, dating back to the 7th century. The temple was originally a small place of worship, but was expanded into a large shrine by the Vijayanagar empire. The temple has many legends and myths associated with it. The Virupaksha Temple is located on the south bank of the Tungabadra river.

question:
Consider the following Pairs: (UPSC Prelims 2009)

Tradition

 State

1. Gatka, a traditional martial art

 Kerala

2. Madhubani, a traditional painting

Bihar

3. Singhey Khababs Sindhu Darshan Festival

 Jammu and Kashmir

Which of the pairs given above is/are correctly matched?
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 3 only
option_3: 2 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Gatka :

  • It is a traditional martial art that originated in the Punjab in the 15th century. It’s a stick-fighting style that uses wooden sticks to imitate swords. The art form is primarily associated with the Sikhs of Punjab and other related ethnic groups, such as Hindkowans.
  • Gatka is a battle technique created by Sikh warriors during the war period of Sikh Gurus. It mainly uses weapons like Kirpan, Talwar (Sword), Katar, Shield, and Sotti (Stick).
  • Gatka is now popular as a sport or sword dance performance art and is often shown during Sikh festivals. In 2023, Gatka was officially included in the 37th National Games, scheduled to be held in Goa in October.

Madhubani painting :

  • It is also known as Mithila art, originated in the Madhubani district of the Mithila region of Bihar, India.
  • The paintings are known for their line drawings filled with bright colors and patterns. They also use natural dyes and pigments for an eye-catching finish.
  • The paintings are traditionally created by women from many communities in the Mithila region. Madhubani is also a major export center for these paintings.
  • Madhubani is a town in the district of the same name in Bihar. The name Madhubani means “Forest of Money”.

The Singhey Khababs Sindhu Festival :

  • It is also known as the Sindhu Darshan Festival, is a three-day festival in Leh, Ladakh, India. It’s celebrated annually on the full moon day of Guru Purnima, which is in June. The festival is held on the banks of the Indus River, which is known as the Sindhu River in India.
  • The festival’s purpose is to promote tourism in the Ladakh region and to highlight the Indus River as a symbol of India’s cultural identity. It’s also considered a symbol of India’s unity and communal harmony.

question: In which State is the Buddhist site Tabo Monastery located? (UPSC Prelims 2009)
option_1: Arunachal Pradesh
option_2: Himachal Pradesh
option_3: Sikkim
option_4: Uttarakhand
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Buddhist site Tabo Monastery is located in the Tabo village of Spiti Valley, Himachal Pradesh, India.
  • Tabo Monastery is an example of vernacular architecture from the Lahaul and Spiti region of Himachal Pradesh. It was founded in 996 CE by the Tibetan Buddhist Lotsawa Rinchen Zangpo on behalf of the king of the western Himalayan Kingdom of Guge, Yeshe-O. It is one of the oldest Buddhist pilgrimage centers in the Trans-Himalayas region.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: (UPSC Prelims 2009)

List-I (Famous

Temple)

List-II

(State)

A. Vidyashankara

1.Andhra Pradesh

B.Rajarani Temple

2.Karnataka

C. Kandariya

Mahadeo Temple

3.Madhya Pradesh

D. Bhimesvara Temple

4.Orissa

Codes:

A B C D
option_1: 2 4 3 1
option_2: 2 3 4 1
option_3: 1 4 3 2
option_4: 1 3 4 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The Vidyashankara Temple:

  • It is a 14th century temple in Sringeri, Karnataka, India. It was built in 1338 AD and is a combination of the Hoysala and Dravidian architectural styles.
  • The temple is made entirely of stone and has six doorways, a richly sculpted plinth, and 12 pillars surrounding the mandapa. The pillars each represent one of the 12 zodiac signs. The temple also has a garbhagriha on the western side, with Vidya Ganapati on one side and Durga on the other.

The Rajarani Temple :

  • It is an 11th-century Hindu temple in Bhubaneswar, India. It's also known as the "love temple" because of its carvings of couples and women. The temple is built in the Kalinga architectural style and is known for its architecture.
  • The temple is located in the Khurda district, northeast of Lingaraj temple. 

The Kandariya Mahadeva Temple :

  • It is a Hindu temple in the Khajuraho Group of Monuments in Madhya Pradesh, India.
  • It's considered one of the best examples of medieval temples in India.
  • The temple was built between 1025-1050 AD. It's the largest and most ornate temple in the Khajuraho complex, with about 870 sculptures. The temple is built in the shape of a steep mountain, with 84 mini shikharas on the roof to represent the minor mountain peaks around Mount Meru. The temple's name means "the Great God of the Cave".

Bhimeshvara Temple :

  • It is located in Nilagunda, in the Davangere district of Karnataka, India. The temple is sometimes confused with other temples in the state.
  • The temple was constructed in the 11th century AD during the rule of the Chalukyas of Kalyana.

question:
Consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2009)

1. The National School of Drama was set up by Sangeet Natak Akademi in 1959.

2. The highest honour conferred by the Sahitya Akademi on a writer is by electing him its Fellow.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • National School of Drama (NSD) was established in 1959 by the Sangeet Natak Akademi. The NSD is a theater training institute in New Delhi, India, and is considered one of the world’s leading theater training institutions. In 1975, the NSD became an independent entity and was registered as an autonomous organization. In 2005, it was granted deemed university status.
  • The NSD offers training in acting, stagecraft, and related subjects. It has four regional centers in addition to its Delhi campus:

1. Gangtok

2. Bengaluru

3. Agartala and

4. Varanasi

  • Sahitya Akademi Fellowship is the highest honor that the Sahitya Akademi, India’s National Academy of Letters, can bestow on a living writer. The fellowship is reserved for “the immortals of literature” and is limited to 21 fellows at any given time.
  • The fellowship was established in 1968. The awardees are selected by a three-member jury and receive a casket containing an engraved copper-plaque and a cheque for Rs. 50,000.

question: There are only two known examples of cave paintings of the Gupta period in ancient India. One of these is paintings of Ajanta caves. Where is the other surviving example of Gupta paintings? (UPSC Prelims 2010)
option_1: Bagh caves
option_2: Ellora caves
option_3: Lomas Rishi cave
option_4: Nasik caves
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The other surviving example of Gupta paintings is the  Bagh caves, which are located in Madhya Pradesh. The Bagh caves are a group of nine rock-cut monuments near Gwalior. They are considered an extension of the Ajanta school, and are similar in design, execution, and decoration. 
  • The paintings display the distinctive Gupta artistic style, with their elegant figures, vibrant colors, attention to detail, and narrative depictions. 
  • The Gupta period is generally regarded as a classic peak and golden age of North Indian art for all the major religious groups. The period saw the emergence of the iconic carved stone deity in Hindu art. 

Ellora Caves :

  • They are a series of 34 rock-cut temples in the Charanandri hills of Maharashtra, India. The caves were built between 600 and 1000 CE and contain inscriptions from the 6th century CE onwards. The caves are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and are also known as verul caves.
  • The caves are a multi-religious complex that showcases the spirit of tolerance and co-existence that was characteristic of ancient India.
  •  The caves are home to three religions: Buddhism, Jainism, and Brahminism, and illustrate the spirit of tolerance that allowed these religions to establish their sanctuaries and communities in one place.

The Lomas Rishi Cave :

  • It is a man-made sanctuary cave in the Barabar Caves complex in the Jehanabad district of Bihar, India. The cave is located on the southern side of the Barabar Hills, about 19 kilometers from Gaya.
  • The Lomas Rishi Cave is one of four Barabar Caves, which are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India. The caves are carved from granite and date back to the 3rd century BC, during the Mauryan Empire. The name “baraabar” means “on par with one another”.
  • The Lomas Rishi Cave has two rooms, an arch-like façade, and an elaborately decorated door with a row of elephants and makara (crocodile) motifs. The entrance is shaped like an elephant’s back. The cave is unfinished and has no inscription of Ashoka.
  • The caves were donated as dwellings to Ajivika ascetics, an ancient Indian religion that vanished after the 14th century.

Nasik caves :

  • They are a group of 23 rock - cut caves located on Trirashmi hill in Nasik, Maharashtra. The caves were carved between the 1st century BCE and the 3rd century CE, and additional sculptures were added up to about the 6th century. The caves reflect the Hinayana sect of Buddhism.
  • The caves are also known as Pandavleni caves or Trirashmi caves. They are locatedon the north face of a hill called Trirasmi in ancient times, 8 km southwest of Nasik town. The caves are built on the Trirasmi hill about 3004 feet above the sea.
  • The Pandavleni Caves were built by Buddhist monks who sought solace and enlightenment in these serene surroundings. The location provides a serene and peaceful atmosphere, making it conducive to meditation and contemplation.

question: The Jain philosophy holds that the world is created and maintained by........... (UPSC Prelims 2011)
option_1: Universal law
option_2: Universal Truth
option_3: Universal Faith
option_4: Universal Soul
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • According to the Jain philosophy, the world is created and maintained by Universal Law. Jainism doesn’t believe in a creator deity. Instead, the Jain doctrine states that the universe and its components have always existed.
  • Jainism, also known as Jain Dharma, is an Indian religion.
  • Jainism traces its spiritual ideas and history through the succession of twenty four tirthankaras (supreme preachers of Dharma), with the first in the current time cycle being Rishabhadeva, whom the tradition holds to have lived millions of years ago, the twenty-third tirthankara Parshvanatha, whom historians date to the 9th century BCE, and the twenty-fourth tirthankara Mahavira, around 600 BCE.
  • Jainism is considered an eternal dharma with the tirthankaras guiding every time cycle of the cosmology. The three main pillars of Jainism are ahimsā (non violence), anekāntavāda (non-absolutism), and aparigraha (asceticism).

Jain monks take five main vows:

1. ahimsā (non-violence),

2. satya (truth),

3. asteya (not stealing),

4. brahmacharya (chastity), and

5. aparigraha (non-possessiveness).

  • These principles have affected Jain culture in many ways, such as leading to a predominantly lacto-vegetarian lifestyle.

question: The Nagara, the Dravida and the Vesara are the........... (UPSC Prelims 2012)
option_1: Three main racial groups of the Indian subcontinent.
option_2: Three main linguistic divisions into which the languages of India can be classified.
option_3: Three main styles of Indian Temple Architecture.
option_4: Three main musical gharanas were prevalent in India.
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Nagara, Dravida, and Vesara are three main styles of Hindu temple architecture in India.

The Nagara style :

  • It is a Hindu style of temple architecture that originated in northern India. It’s popular in Northern, Western, and Eastern India, especially in the regions around Malwa, Rajputana, and Kalinga.

Here are some features of the Nagara style:

1. Raised plinth :

The temple is built on a raised stone platform with steps leading up to it.

2. Garbha griha :

The garbha griha, or sanctum sanctorum, is the most sacred part of the temple. It’s a perfect square and houses the image or idol of the main deity.

3. Shikhara :

The most distinguishable aspect of Nagara style temples is the towering shikhara, or “mountain peak”, that towers over thegarbha griha. The earliest temples have just one shikhara, but later temples have several. The garbhagriha is always located directly under the tallest tower.

4. Kalasha :

The shikhara is topped by a bulbous finial called a kalasha.

5. Plan :

The plan of a Nagara style of temple is four sided, but the whole temple plan could be oblong.

6. Subdivisions :

There are many subdivisions of nagara temples depending on the shape of the shikhara.

Here are some examples of Nagara style temples:

  1. Kandariya Mahadeva Temple: Lo cated in Khajuraho, Madhya Pradesh
  2. Jagdish Temple: Located in Udaipur, Rajasthan
  3. Khajuraho temple complex: A UNESCO World Heritage Site
  4. Konark Sun Temple: A UNESCO World Heritage Site
  5. Mahabodhi Temple: A UNESCO World Heritage Site
  6. Sun Temple in Modhera, Gujarat: An example of Nagara-style temple architecture
  7. Ossian Temple in Gujarat: An exam ple of Nagara-style temple architecture

Dravidian architecture :

  • It is a style of temple architecture that originated in South India during the Gupta period. It’s one of the three main styles of Indian temple architecture, along with the Nagara and Vesara styles.

Here are some features of Dravidian architecture:

1. Compound wall: The temple is sur rounded by a compound wall.

2. Gopuram: The entrance gateway is located in the center of the front wall.

3. Vimana: The main temple tower is a stepped pyramid that rises geomet rically. The vimana is also known as the gopuram or shikhara. It’s typically adorned with intricate carvings and sculptures, and often has multiple tiers.

4. Pillars and pilasters: The temple has pillars and pilasters.

5. Curved top: The temple has a curved top.

6. Boundary walls: The temple has boundary walls.

7. Water tank: The temple enclosure has a water tank.

8. Panchayatan style: The temple grounds are designed in the panchayatan style, with a main temple and four smaller shrines.

  • Some examples of Dravidian temples include the Brihadeeshwara temple at Thanjavur and the Gangaikondacholapuram temple.

The Vesara style of temple architecture :

  • It is a combination of the Dravidian and Nagara styles of temple architecture. It’s a hybrid form of Indian temple architecture, with the plan and shape of South Indian temples and the details of North Indian temples.
  • The Vesara style is said to have originated in the mid-seventh century AD with the later Chalukya rulers. The word “Vesara” is derived from the Sanskrit word vishra, which means “an area to walk for a long time”.

Some features of the Vesara style include:

1. Dravidian vimana

2. Nagara-type faceted walls

3. Selective merging of the Nagara and Dravida orders

4. A star-shaped plan

5. Projecting angles in circles with centers in the middle of the shrine and mandap

6.  Pillars, doorways, ceilings are decorated with intricate carvings

7. A usually pyramidal tower, similar to the Dravida style, but may lack the stepped appearance seen in pure Dravida architecture

  • Some famous temples of Vesara style are the Kailashnath temple, Ellora, Chennakesava temple, Virupaksha temple, and Ladkhan temple.

question:
With reference to Dhrupad, one of the major traditions of India that has been kept alive for centuries, Which of the following statements are correct? (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Dhrupad originated and developed in the Rajput kingdoms during the Mughal period

2. Dhrupad is primarily a devotional and spiritual music

3. Dhrupad alap uses Sanskrit syllables from Mantras.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 and 2 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1, 2 and 3
option_4: None of the above is correct.
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Dhrupad did not originate in the Rajput kingdoms during the Mughal period.
  • Dhrupad is a major Indian tradition that has been kept alive for centuries. It is a spiritual and devotional music that originated from the chanting of Vedic hymns and mantras.

 Dhrupad is performed in two parts:

1. Alap and

2. Bandish.

  • In the Alap, the singer uses syllables from Sanskrit Mantras.
  • Dhrupad is said to be the oldest vocal music, with origins dating back to the 16th century at Akbar’s Mughal court. Some of its most important traits include the sung lyrics and adherence to rhythm and melody.

question:
How do you distinguish between Kuchipudi and Bharatanatyam dances? (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Dancers occasionally speaking dialogues is found in Kuchipudi dance but not in Bharatnatyam

2. Dancing on the brass plate by keeping the feet on its edges is a feature of Bharatnatyam but Kuchipudi dance does not have such a form of movements.

Which of the pairs given above is/are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 or 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Dancers in Kuchipudi occasionally speak dialogues, but Bharatanatyam dancers do not.

Here are some other differences between the two dances:

1. Dialogue: dancers Kuchipudi sometimes speak dialogues, which are called "Sutras" or "Abhinaya Darpana". These dialogues are usually used to tell stories or convey emotions. Bharatanatyam dancers do not speak dialogues.

2. Brass plate: Kuchipudi dancers dance on a brass plate, but Bharatanatyam dancers do not.

3. Origin: Bharatanatyam originated in the temples of South India.

4. Facial expressions: Bharatanatyam or facial relies on abhinaya, expressions.

  • Bharatanatyam is a dance form that  focuses on expressive storytelling through body movements, gestures, and facial expressions. The Bharatanatyam style is known for its fixed upper torso, bent legs, and flexed knees combined with footwork.

question:
With reference to the history of Ancient India, which of the following was/were common to both Buddhism and Jainism? (UPSC Prelims 2012)

1. Avoidance of extremities of penance and enjoyment

2. Indifference to the authority of Vedas

3. Denial of the efficacy of rituals

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Buddhism and Jainism share many similarities, including:

1. Karma :

  • Both religions believe in karma, which is the attachment of positive and negative forces to the soul.

2. Salvation :

  • Both religions believe that salvation can only be achieved through nirvana.

3. Helping others :

  • Both religions believe in helping others and teaching their values to others.

4. Materialistic world :

  • Both religions teach to stay away from the materialistic world and desires.

5. Moral lives :

  • Both religions give importance to the moral lives of people.

6. Harm to humans :

  • The teachings of Jainism and Buddhism condemn all sorts of harm meted to humans or living beings.

7. Non-violence :

  • Both religions believe and propagate non violence, good social conduct, charity, and generosity.

8. Atheism :

  • Both religions are atheistic in the sense that they don’t believe in there’s a creator god or any super being.

9. Common background :

  • Both religions have a common background associated with the Aryan Culture.

10. Against Veda :

  • Both religions were against Vedas and the Vedic culture as well as against Brahmanism.

11. Denial of efficiency of rituals :

  • Both religions deny the efficiency of rituals.

12. Only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge :

  • Both religions believe that only those who left their homes could gain true knowledge.

13. Non injury to animal life.

Buddhism and Jainism also share some other similarities, including:

1. Both religions emerged as a new religious ideas from late 6th century BCE.

2. Both religions preached Satya, Ahimsa, Brahmacharya, and detachment from the materialistic world.

question: Lord Buddha’s image is sometimes shown with the hand gesture called “Bhumisparsha mudra”. It symbolises......... (UPSC Prelims 2012)
option_1: Buddha’s calling of the Earth to watch over Mara and to prevent Mara from disturbing his meditation.
option_2: Buddha’s calling of the Earth to witness his purity and chastity despite the temptations of Mara.
option_3: Buddha’s reminder to his followers that they all arise from Earth and finally dissolve into the Earth, and thus this life is transitory.
option_4: Both statements (a)and (b) are correct in this context.
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Bhumisparsha mudra is a hand gesture that symbolizes the Buddha’s enlightenment. It’s formed by extending the five fingers of the right hand to touch the ground. The left hand rests on the lap.
  • The mudra represents the moment of the Buddha’s enlightenment, when he summoned the earth goddess, Sthavara, to witness his attainment of enlightenment. It also symbolizes the Buddha’s calling of the Earth to witness his purity and chastity despite the temptations of Mara.
  • The Bhumisparsha mudra also commemorates the Buddha’s victory over the temptation by the demon-king Mara. In Buddhist art, mudras are a set of hand gestures and finger positions that serve as symbols representing the Buddha’s various roles and states of mind.
  • Mudras are symbolic or ritual gestures or poses in Hinduism, Jainism, and Buddhism. They are spiritual gestures employed in the iconography and spiritual practice of Indian religions.

Some mudras of Buddha :

1. Dhyana mudra :

  • One of the five common mudras in Buddhist art. It refers to the prince Siddhartha meditating under the pipal tree before he achieved enlightenment.

2. Abhaya mudra :

  • This mudra is made with the open palm of the right hand, extending outwards at the chest level or slightly higher. Translated from Sanskrit as fearlessness, it signifies “stop”. This means to end fears and phobias that can cloud your mind.

3. Dharmachakra mudra :

  • This mudra is a reference to the Buddha’s first sermon at Sarnath. It gets its name from its association with the eponymous dharmachakra or “wheel of law”.

4. Varada mudra :

  • Also known as the “Favourable Mudra” or the Mudra of granting wishes/mercy. This mudra has also been depicted in various Buddha statues.

5. Vitarka mudra :

  • This mudra symbolizes the pursuit of knowledge. Vastu Shastra suggests that placing a Buddha statue with the Vitarka mudra on your library, work, or study desk can help you attain excellence in work and academic pursuits.

6. Anjali mudra :

  • This mudra denotes welcome, prayer, and devotion motion.

7. Uttarabodhi mudra :

  • This mudra denotes the supreme enlightenment through connecting oneself with divine universal energy. It is performed with the help of both the hands, which are placed at the heart with the index fingers touching and pointing upwards and the remaining fingers intertwined.

question:
Consider the following Bhakti Saints............ (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. Dadu Dayal 

2. Guru Nanak

3. Tyagaraja

Who among the above was/were preaching when the Lodi dynasty fell and Babur took over?
option_1: 1 and 3
option_2: 2 only
option_3: 2 and 3
option_4: 1 and 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Guru Nanak Dev was born in 1469. Babur defeated Ibrahim Lodi in 1526.
  • When Babur took over and established the Mughal dynasty Nanak was travelling to different parts of India and preaching Sikhism.
  • Babur once met Guru Nanak during one of his travel.

Some important Bhakti saints:

Ramanuja :

  • A well-known Bhakti saint from Tamil Nadu in the 11th century. He is known for his “Vishishtadvaita” philosophy and preached about salvation through devotion to Lord Vishnu.

Vallabhacharya :

  • A prominent Bhakti saint and philosopher. His devotees built a temple in Champaranya, where he was born, which is now a pilgrimage site.

Guru Nanak :

  • A highly influential Bhakti saint who incorporated ideas from both Hinduism and Islam. His teachings include the importance of reciting God’s name with devotion and the need for a Guru for salvation.

Eknath :

  • A popular and important Bhakti saint from Maharashtra who lived in the 16th century. He was also a prolific writer and social reformer.

Ramananda :

  • A 15th century Bhakti saint from Prayag who is considered the first great Bhakti saint of North India. He believed in two principles: perfect love for God and human brotherhood.

Dadu Dayal :

  • A well-known Bhakti saint from the Hindu tradition of North India. His compositions aimed to bring Hinduism and Islam closer together.

Mirabai :

  • A popular Bhakti saint and devotee of Lord Krishna. She was a medieval Hindu spiritual poet from a royal family in Rajasthan. Her hymns dedicated to Lord Krishna are still popular and revered in north India.
  • Bhakti saints often expressed their sentiments through song, poetry, and music, and often attracted thousands of followers. They largely rejected the hereditary caste system and its emphasis on prescribed ritual. Instead, they stressed the need for morality, purity of heart, and an attitude of selfless service.

question:
With reference to the history of philosophical thought in India, consider the following statements regarding Sankhya school........... (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. Sankhya does not accept the theory of rebirth or transmigration of the soul.

2. Sankhya holds that it is the self knowledge that leads to liberation and not any exterior influence or agent.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 only
option_3: Both 1 and 2
option_4: Neither 1 nor 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Sankhya is one of the six orthodox schools of Indian philosophy and accepts the theory of rebirth.
  • Sankhya believes that the individual soul, or Purusha, is eternal and unchanging. It also believes that the soul moves from one body to another after death. However, Sankhya distinguishes between the soul and the material body, which is considered temporary and subject to change.
  • Sankhya also believes in the “Law of Karma” and that the attainment of Moksha/Liberation is the highest goal of human struggle. Sankhya believes that self-knowledge leads to liberation and that liberation is attained through the knowledge of the true nature of the self. Sankhya is believed to have been founded by the Vedic sage Kapila around 550 BCE.

question: Some Buddhist rock-cut caves are called Chaityas, while the others are called Viharas. What is the difference between the two? (UPSC Prelims 2013)
option_1: Vihara is a place of worship, while Chaitya is the dwelling place of the monks.
option_2: Chaitya is a place of worship, while Vihara is the dwelling place of the monks.
option_3: Chaitya is the stupa at the far end of the cave, while Vihara is the hall axial to it.
option_4: There is no material difference between the two.
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In Buddhist rock cut cave temples, a chaitya is a place of worship, and a vihara is a residence for monks. Chaityas are stupa monument halls, and viharas are monasteries with residence halls for monks.
  • Chaityas are usually located at the center of the apse, and viharas are located on three sides of the monastery.
  • Chaityas are places of worship, and viharas are prayer halls. The word “chaitya” comes from the word “chita”, which is a mound of ashes formed by the cremation of a dead body. Eventually, it came to mean the earth mound heaped over the ashes or relics of a saint.
  • Viharas are dwellings of one or two stories, fronted by a pillared veranda. They contain large windows above a decorated main door and long rows of pillars.

question: Which one of the following describes best the concept of Nirvana in Buddhism? (UPSC Prelims 2013)
option_1: The extinction of the flame of desire
option_2: The complete annihilation of self
option_3: A state of bliss and rest
option_4: A mental stage beyond all comprehension
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The concept of Nirvana in Buddhism is best described as the extinction of the flame of desire.
  • Nirvana is the earliest and most common term used to describe the goal of the Buddhist path. It means the extinction of craving, or the extinction of the fires of attachment, aversion, and ignorance.
  • In the Buddhist tradition, Nirvana is described as the extinguishing of the fires that cause rebirths and associated suffering.

The Buddhist texts identify these “three fires” or “three poisons” as:

1. Raga: Greed, sensuality

2. Dvesha: Aversion, hate

3. Avidya or moha: Ignorance, delusion

  • Nirvana is not a place but an experience and could be attained in this life.

 The four stages of achieving Nirvana are:

1. Stream enterer,

2. Once returner,

3. Non returner,

4. Fully enlightened.

  • The stages are determined by how many of the 10 fetters (sanyojanas) the practitioner has eradicated.
  • The Bodhi Tree is a Buddhist symbol of enlightenment. It represents the place where the Buddha attained Nirvana or Enlightenment. The original Bodhi tree is a fig that stands in Bhod Gaya, India, and is a sacred pilgrimage site.

question:
Which of the following statements is/are applicable to Jain doctrine? (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. The surest way of annihilating Karma is to practise penance.

2. Every object, even the smallest particle, has a soul.

3. Karma is the bane of the soul and must be ended.

Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 1 and 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • According to Jain doctrines, the surest way to annihilate karma is to practice penance. Penance is a means to purify the soul and eradicate karmas. It involves self-discipline, self-control, and self-mortification.
  • According to Jain teachings, the cycle of birth and rebirth is shaped through karma.
  • Asceticism and penance are required to free oneself from the cycle of karma. This can be achieved only by renouncing the world; therefore, monastic existence is a necessary condition of salvation.
  • According to Jainism, souls exist in all living things, including plants, animals, and humans. Jain doctrine also states that souls exist in non-living entities, such as rocks, water, and even the tiniest particles.
  • According to Jainism, karma is a subtle matter that infiltrates the soul and obscures its natural qualities. It’s also thought of as a kind of pollution that taints the soul with various colors.

question:
In the context of the cultural history of India, a pose in dance and dramatics called ‘Tribhanga’ has been a favourite of Indian artists from ancient times till today. (UPSC Prelims 2013)

Which one of the following statements best describes this pose?
option_1: One leg is bent and the body is slightly but oppositely curved at the waist and neck.
option_2: Facial expressions, hand gestures and make-up are combined to symbolize certain epic or historic characters.
option_3: Movements of body, face and hands are used to express oneself or to tell a story.
option_4: A little smile, slightly curved waist and certain hand gestures are emphasized to express the feelings of love or eroticism.
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Tribhanga is a standing body position in traditional Indian art and classical dance forms. It is a three-fold bending of the body, with the torso deflecting in one direction and the head and hips deflecting in the opposite direction. The body is oppositely curved at the waist and neck, giving it a gentle “S” shape.
  • The word Tribhanga comes from Sanskrit, with tri meaning “three” and bhanga meaning “attitude” or “posture”. It is considered the most graceful and sensual of the Odissi positions.
  • Tribhanga is also used in some traditional Indian sculptures. It has been a favorite of Indian artists from ancient times.

question:
Consider the following historical places: (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. Ajanta Caves

2. Lepakshi Temple

3. Sanchi Stupa

Which of the above places is/are also known for mural paintings?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 1 and 2 only
option_3: 1, 2 and 3
option_4: None
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

Mural Paintings :

  • The mural is the only form of painting that is truly three-dimensional since it modifies and partakes a given space.
  • Mural paintings are applied on drywall with the major use of egg, yolk, oil, etc.
  • Notable examples : Ajanta Caves, Bagh Caves, Sittanavasal Caves, Armamalai Cave (Tamil Nadu), Kailasa temple (Ellora Caves).
  • Murals from this period depict mainly religious themes of Buddhist, Jain, and Hindu.
  • Depict a large number of incidents from the life of the Buddha (Jataka Tales).
  • Exclusively Buddhist, excepting decorative patterns on the ceilings and the pillars.

The Ajanta Caves :

  • They are a series of 29 rock-cut Buddhist caves in the Sahyadri ranges of Maharashtra,India.
  • The caves were excavated from around 200 BC to 650 AD and are considered to be among the earliest Buddhist architectures. The caves are located on the Waghora river, near Aurangabad, in a horseshoe-shaped bend of rock surface that overlooks the river. The valley’s location provided a calm environment for Buddhist monks during the rainy seasons.
  • The caves are known for their carvings and sculptures that depict the life of Buddha, as well as their frescoes. The caves are considered to be the beginning of classical Indian art, and are an example of ancient Buddhist rock-cut architecture.
  • The caves are divided into two phases and are numbered thirty in total. Four of the caves were used as prayer halls, and 25 were used as residential caves.
  • The Ajanta Caves were discovered in 1819 by British Army officers hunting tigers in the gorge of the Waghora River.

Lepakshi Temple:

  • It is located in Andhra Pradesh, India, is a 16th-century temple dedicated to Lord Shiva. It is known for its intricate carvings and mural paintings.
  • The mural paintings at Lepakshi Temple depict scenes from Hindu mythology and showcase the exquisite craftsmanship of the Vijayanagara Empire. These paintings are characterized by vibrant colors, intricate detailing, and a unique blend of South Indian and Vijayanagara art styles.

Sanchi Stupa :

  • It is located in Madhya Pradesh, India, is one of the oldest stone structures in India and is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It was built by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE to enshrine the relics of Lord Buddha. While Sanchi Stupa is primarily known for its architectural significance and Buddhist symbolism, it does not have mural paintings.

question:
With reference to the history of Indian rock-cut architecture, consider the following statements: (UPSC Prelims 2013)

1. The caves at Badami are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India.

2. The Barabar rock-cut caves were originally made for Ajivikas by Emperor Chandragupta Maurya.

3. At Ellora, caves were made for different faiths.

Which of the statements given above is/ are correct?
option_1: 1 only
option_2: 2 and 3 only
option_3: 3 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • No, the caves at Badami are not the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India. The Barabar Caves in Bihar are the oldest surviving rock-cut caves in India, dating from the 3rd century BCE.
  • The Barabar Caves were built for the Ajivikas by Emperor Ashoka, the grandson of Emperor Chandragupta Maurya. Some of the caves have Ashokan inscriptions and are located in the Bela Ganj Block of Gaya district, Bihar, India.
  • The Badami Caves are a Shaivite cave with carvings of an 18-armed dancing Shiva, a two-handed Ganesha, Mahishasura Mardini, Ardha Nareeshwara, and Shankarnarayana. The ceiling is adorned by a serpent motif and other carved figures. Badami is a town in the Bagalkot district of Karnataka, India. It was the capital of the Badami Chalukyas from 540 to 757.
  • Ellora caves are a complex of Hindu (Caves 13-29), Buddhist (Caves 1-12) and Jain (Caves 30-34) monuments. These were built during the Rashtrakuta dynasty. It was designated as a World heritage site By UNESCO

question: Chaitra, one of the national calendars based on the Saka Era, corresponds to which one of the following dates of the Gregorian calendar in a normal year of 365 days? (UPSC Prelims 2014)
option_1: 22nd March (or 21st March)
option_2: 15th May (or 16th May)
option_3: 21st March (or 30th March)
option_4: 21st April (or 20th April)
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Saka era is believed to have been founded by King Kanishka of the Kushan dynasty in 78 AD.
  • The Saka calendar has 12 months, each with 30–31 days, and 365 days in total. The calendar is based on a luni solar reckoning of time and follows the tropical Zodiac signs. The first month of the Saka Samvat is Chaitra, which begins on March 22.During a leap year, Chaitra begins on March 21.
  • The Saka calendar is similar to the Gregorian calendar, which also has 12 months and 365 days.
  • The Indian National Calendar, based on the Saka era, was adopted by the Parliament of India in 1957. The calendar starts from the month of Chaitra, which falls on March 22 each year, except for leap years, when it falls on March 21. The calendar has a duration of 365 days.

The months of the Saka calendar are:

1. Chaitra: March 21–April 20

2. Vaishakha: April 21–May 21

3. Jyeshtha: May 22–June 21

4. Ashadha: June 22–July 22

5. Shravana: July 23–August 22

6. Bhadra: August 22–September 22

7. Ashwin: September 23–October 22

8. Kartika: October 23–November 21

9. Agrahayana: November 22–Decem ber 21

10. Pausha: December 22–January 20

11. Magha : January 21 - February 19

12. Phalguna. : February 20 - March 21

question:
With reference to Buddhist history, tradition and culture in India, consider the following pairs: (UPSC Prelims 2014)

 

Famous Shrine

 Location
